retrieved_docs.title: Subconjunctival Hemorrhage -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Population-based studies reveal sex-specific differences in the incidence of SCH, although results vary by geography and study methodology. Overall, no major sex discrepancy has been established. Traumatic SCH occurs more frequently in younger male individuals, likely reflecting greater involvement in heavy labor and higher participation in aggressive physical activities.

retrieved_docs.title: Assessment and Management of Traumatic Injuries in Older Adults -- Pathophysiology -- Hematologic
retrieved_docs.content: Bone marrow mass may also decrease with age. Fat replaces marrow, and hematopoietic reserves decline. The marrow itself also becomes less sensitive to stimulatory hormones. The functionality of the red cells, platelets, and leukocytes all develop qualitative defects even though, quantitatively, they may be normal.

retrieved_docs.title: Assessment and Management of Traumatic Injuries in Older Adults -- Pathophysiology -- Genitourinary
retrieved_docs.content: Neurohumoral stimulation of the kidneys also declines with age. The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system is downregulated, making the host less responsive to hypovolemia and salt retention. As renal function declines, the kidney becomes less responsive to hypoxia, leading to decreased erythropoietin production and anemia. The ability of vitamin D to undergo hydroxylation also decreases, putting older patients at higher risk for osteomalacia and osteoporotic fractures.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Trauma -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Acute traumatic coagulopathy ensues immediately after massive trauma when shock, hypoperfusion, and vascular damage are present. The mechanisms for this include activation of protein C, endothelial glycocalyx disruption, depletion of fibrinogen, and platelet dysfunction. Factors such as hypothermia and acidaemia may amplify the endogenous coagulopathy and often accompany trauma. Hemodilution, hypothermia, and acidaemia are 3 major factors that subsequently lead to trauma-induced coagulopathy and, as such, are known as the "classic trauma triad." All these factors culminate in decreased clot strength, autoheparinization, and hyperfibrinolysis. This increases the mortality and morbidity ratios significantly and must be treated vigilantly. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute and Chronic Mental Health Trauma -- Pathophysiology -- Neurotransmitters
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) is released from the pituitary after stimulation from CRH, and ACTH acts on the adrenal glands, producing and systemically releasing corticosteroids. Release of ACTH from CRH stimulation is generally increased in women diagnosed with PTSD, whereas ACTH release is generally decreased in men; these findings may vary.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurogenic Shock -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis in cases of neurogenic shock is influenced by various factors, with 2 critical elements mentioned below. Spinal cord injury: As assessed through the American Spinal Injury Association (ASIA) scale, the severity of spinal cord injury significantly influences the overall prognosis. Treatment response: The individual's response to treatment and management strategies significantly influences the expected outcomes. In addition to the factors mentioned above, an individual's prognosis in cases of neurogenic shock can be further influenced by several significant factors, as provided below. Neurological deficits: The existence and severity of neurological deficits at the initial assessment can have a pivotal role in shaping the overall prognosis. Age: Age is a determining factor, as younger patients often display a more favorable prognostic potential for recovery and adaptation.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemorrhagic Shock -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Trauma remains a leading cause of death worldwide, with approximately half of these attributed to hemorrhage. In the United States in 2001, trauma was the third leading cause of death overall and the leading cause of death in those aged 1 to 44. While trauma spans all demographics, it disproportionately affects the young, with 40% of injuries occurring in ages 20 to 39 years by one country’s account. Of this 40%, the greatest incidence was in the 20 to 24-year-old range. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Trauma -- Pathophysiology -- Penetrating Trauma
retrieved_docs.content: A hypovolemic shock from penetrating trauma is 1 of the most feared consequences because it can result in death if not treated promptly. A patient in hypovolemic shock typically presents with hypotension, tachycardia, tachypnea, and cold skin. Acute blood loss means less circulating volume and decreased organ perfusion, dependent on arterial pressure. The most vulnerable organs of decreased perfusion are the kidneys, brain, heart, liver, and colon. The body implements several processes to maintain adequate oxygenation to the previously mentioned organs. All of which the mainstay is the autonomic nervous system. One goal is to maintain cardiac output (CO), defined by heart rate x stroke volume, which is achieved by activation of the sympathetic nervous system (SNS), causing the release of plasma catecholamines, such as vasopressin and norepinephrine, increasing the heart rate and thus CO. Another goal is to increase systemic vascular resistance (SVR); via the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system. Renin is released by juxtaglomerular cells in response to decreased systemic blood pressure and NaCl delivery to the macula densa. From there, renin converts to angiotensin I, angiotensin II, and aldosterone. Regarding hypovolemia, angiotensin II acts on blood vessels, causing vasoconstriction and on the hypothalamus to cause the release of the anti-diuretic hormone, leading to water reabsorption at the collecting ducts. Additionally, angiotensin II limits baroreceptor reflex bradycardia, further aiding in the maintenance of CO. [6] Each cascade is a component of the body’s response to hemorrhage to avoid shock.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemorrhagic Shock -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The body’s compensation varies by cardiopulmonary comorbidities, age, and vasoactive medications. Due to these factors, heart rate and blood pressure responses are extremely variable and, therefore, cannot be relied upon as the sole means of diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Care Principles -- Function -- Circulation
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of hemorrhage depends on the source and severity of the bleed. For example, a superficial bleed may only require gauze while others may need prolonged direct pressure; a open fracture of the femur with vascular disruption may require a tourniquet and immediate surgical intervention. Patients might also need external compression devices, such as a pelvic binder, to tamponade internal bleeds. Often, patients require fluid replacement to maintain appropriate perfusion pressures. The amount of blood loss determines the level of fluid resuscitation and the use of blood products (red blood cells, platelets, and fresh frozen plasma). According to ATLS, hemorrhage gets grouped into Classes I to IV, with Class IV being the most severe and require the most aggressive treatment; this may include vasopressors and massive transfusion protocols depending on the facility’s policy. Factors that define the patient's class status include changes in heart rate, blood pressure, urine output, and GCS, among others. In conjunction with the treatment modalities mentioned before, it is important to know a patient’s hemoglobin, hematocrit, and INR/PTT levels, along with their anticoagulation status, blood type, and Rh factor status. The ATLS hemorrhagic shock classes are described below [10] [11] [12] [2] [13] :

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2226
retrieved_docs.content: Traumatisch hersenoedeem is a specific subtype of أوديما دماغية, meaning that traumatic brain edema is classified under the broader condition of cerebral edema. As a form of أوديما دماغية, Traumatisch hersenoedeem involves swelling of brain tissue that results from head injury, leading to increased intracranial pressure and potential neurological impairment. Recognizing Traumatisch hersenoedeem as a subset of أوديما دماغية helps clinicians understand its pathophysiology within the context of general cerebral edema, guiding appropriate diagnostic evaluation and targeted therapeutic interventions to reduce brain swelling and protect neurological function.

An inverse‑isa relationship clarifies that نزيف حديثي الولادة (neonatal bleeding) is a specific type of نزيف مخي وليدي (intracerebral bleeding in the newborn). Birth trauma is mapped to نزيف مخي وليدي, reinforcing the connection between traumatic delivery events and intracerebral hemorrhage. Cerebral injury due to birth trauma is positioned as an inverse‑isa of birth trauma, indicating that cerebral injury is a specific manifestation of the broader traumatic event during delivery. Finally, life‑stage taxonomy places Infancy as an inverse‑isa of the Period of life between birth and death, confirming that infancy is a distinct sub‑period within the overall human lifespan. Collectively, these hierarchical and multilingual relationships integrate gestational age, birth outcomes, fetal growth disturbances, a spectrum of neonatal infections, genetic syndromes, bleeding events, various forms of jaundice, and traumatic injuries, providing a comprehensive framework for

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 691
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma is identified as a specific type of birth trauma. In the context of neonatal and fetal health, birth trauma is mapped to intracerebral bleeding that can occur in the fetus or newborn. Consequently, when a birth trauma event leads to intracerebral hemorrhage, it may result in a cerebral injury that is directly attributable to the traumatic birth process. Understanding this link highlights the importance of recognizing and managing intracerebral bleeding in newborns as a potential cause of cerebral injury stemming from birth trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 734
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is a broad category of maternal complications that encompasses several specific conditions. It has as child entities postpartum hemorrhage (نزف ما بعد الولادة), failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض), and fetal death (موت الجنين). In addition, this category is classified as obstetric cervical tear (تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم) and pelvic obstetric hematoma (ورم دموي توليدي حوضي). Together, these interrelated complications illustrate the diverse and serious events that can arise during the postpartum period and influence the clinical management of the mother.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 357
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma is a specific form of birth trauma, and birth trauma is closely linked to neonatal intracranial hemorrhage (نزيف مخي وليدي). Both cerebral injury due to birth trauma and neonatal intracranial hemorrhage are classified as members of the broader category “Disorders of the newborn (SMQ)”. In addition, the delivery complication described as “ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}” (birth at the maximum degree of breech presentation with unspecified weight) is also included in the “Disorders of the newborn (SMQ)” grouping. Together, these relationships highlight that severe breech deliveries and birth‑related trauma can lead to serious cerebral injuries such as intracranial hemorrhage in the newborn, emphasizing the importance of careful obstetric management and neonatal monitoring.
